narrative: Dear [Student's Name], imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'd conquer the bar exam. Your story starts strong with a solid 4th decile ranking in your first year of law school, but it was your 2nd decile standing by your third year that cast the longest shadow—that dip became the biggest signal to the model that challenges were mounting, pulling hardest toward the risk of not passing the bar. Layered on top was your first-year performance in the 4th decile, reinforcing that early promise hadn't fully held, making it the second strongest factor tipping the scales toward failure. Your LSAT score of 23 pointed in the same direction, suggesting the analytical edge needed for bar success might be a stretch, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 added to that narrative of steady but not standout academic momentum. Coming from a middle-income family influenced the picture too, as the model saw it as less buffer against the rigors ahead, and your Asian background wove in as another thread leaning toward higher risk in this predictive tale. Even your gender played a subtle role in that direction, though your choice to go full-time stood out as the one bright spark, gently nudging toward success amid the rest. Together, these elements painted a composite where the odds of bar passage hovered at just 44%, leading to the tough call to reject your application—not a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven glimpse of the hurdles the model foresaw. You've got resilience in committing full-time, and that's a strength to build on wherever your path leads next.
